vignette.name: VOID Link Audio
vignette.p: VOID Link Audio is a Max package that brings Ableton Link Audio streaming directly into the Max patcher.

It exposes two MSP objects, void.linkaudio.send~ and void.linkaudio.receive~, that let any Max patch publish and receive audio over the local Link Audio network alongside Ableton Live, TouchDesigner, VCV Rack, and any other Link Audio host on the same LAN. Both objects also expose the live Link session timing (tempo, phase, transport) as audio-rate signals, making the shared session clock directly available for sample-accurate scheduling and modulation in the patch.
Built on top of Ableton Link Audio (GPL-2.0-or-later, by Ableton AG). Not endorsed by Ableton.
The core idea behind VOID Link Audio is simple and explicit:

ul.li: any application publishes one or more named audio channels
any application subscribes to a named audio channel
the live Link session timing flows alongside the audio

vignette.p: Channels are identified by name and discovered automatically over the local network. Audio flows in real time, sample-accurate, and in lock-step with the shared Ableton Link tempo and beat.

Publishes one or two channels of audio onto the network under a user-defined channel name. The number of inlets is fixed at creation time via the @stereo attribute (@stereo 1 for two inlets, @stereo 0 for one). The advertised channel is named via @channel and tagged with the local @peer name. In addition to publishing audio, it outputs the current Link tempo, phase and transport as audio-rate signals so the patch generating the audio can synchronize itself to the shared session.

Subscribes to an audio channel published on the network and outputs the received signal on its left and right outlets. The target is addressed by @frompeer (the source peer's name) and @fromchannel (the channel name on that peer). The same timing outlets (tempo, phase, transport) are exposed so a downstream patch can react to the session timing of the audio it receives. Subscription is automatic and resilient: when the polling clock is enabled, the object continually retries to match the requested target against currently advertised channels and transparently reconnects after peer drops.
Both objects expose three audio-rate signal outlets reflecting the shared Link session timing:

ul.li: tempo~ - session BPM, slowly varying
phase~ - sawtooth in [0, quantum), wraps at quantum boundaries
transport~ - 0.0 when stopped, 1.0 when playing

vignette.p: These are sampled at the start of each DSP buffer from captureAudioSessionState() and advanced linearly across the buffer using beatsPerSample = tempo / 60 / sampleRate. They keep producing meaningful values regardless of subscription or publication state, as long as the underlying Link session is alive. Use the wrap of phase~ to detect downbeats, drive phase-locked LFOs, gate triggers on transport~, and so on.
vignette.h2: Tempo and Transport as R/W Attributes

ul.li: Reading queries the session live, so external changes (from Live, another peer, another Max patch) are reflected immediately when the attributes are queried.
Writing commits a new tempo or transport state back to the session, propagating to all peers. Transport propagation relies on Start/Stop Sync, which is enabled internally on the shared session.

The polling clock periodically refreshes the attribute storage from the session and notifies observers (attrui, pattr, inspector) via object_attr_touch, so external tempo/transport changes eventually appear in the UI without manual refresh.

vignette.p: Every Link Audio participant on the network is identified by two pieces of information:

ul.li: a peer name, advertised once per process
one or more channel names, advertised per stream

vignette.p: A subscriber identifies its target by the pair (frompeer, fromchannel). Specifying frompeer is essential when multiple peers publish a channel with the same name. Channel names are case-sensitive.

Within a single Max instance, the local peer identity is shared across all VOID Link Audio objects (Link Audio uses a single shared peer name per process). On the Send side this identity is set via @peer. On the Receive side, no local peer name is exposed: the Receive only cares about the source's name via @frompeer.

Both objects ship with an internal Max clock that periodically re-applies their state. This makes the network connection robust against transient setup issues, naturally handles peers appearing and disappearing on the network, and refreshes the tempo / transport attribute mirrors when the session is changed externally.
The clock is controlled by two attributes:

ul.li: @poll 0|1 enables or disables the clock
@pollinterval <ms> sets the tick period (50 to 60000 ms)

vignette.p: With @poll 1 (the default), there is generally no need to manage subscriptions, retries, or attribute refresh by hand.
Both objects expose a status dictionary on their dumpout. The dictionary contains a complete snapshot of the object state, including:

ul.li: connection flags (enabled, audio_enabled, subscribed/publishing)
peer count and the list of channels currently visible on the network
stream parameters (sample rate, channel count)
traffic counters (frames received/published, drops, no-buffer events)
Link timing values (tempo, transport, beat, phase, quantum)

vignette.p: A fresh dictionary is dumped automatically whenever a meaningful state change occurs (subscription gained/lost, peer count changed, channel list changed) and on demand by sending the info message. Editing attributes does not spam the dumpout.
Route the dictionary through dict.view for inspection, dict.unpack for fan-out, or route for selective handling.
VOID Link Audio is fully compatible with any host that implements the Ableton Link Audio protocol, including:

ul.li: Ableton Live (Link Audio In / Link Audio Out track inputs)
TouchDesigner (Link Audio Send / Receive CHOPs)
VCV Rack (Void Link Audio Send / Receive modules)
any VST3 / AU host (Structure Void's VoidLinkAudio plugins)
any other Max patch using the same package

vignette.p: Streams are exchanged on the local network with sample-accurate timing. Audio published from one host can be received by an arbitrary number of subscribers simultaneously.
VOID Link Audio aims to be:

ul.li: explicit - you choose what to publish, you choose what to receive
resilient - auto-poll handles transient network state silently
observable - the dumpout dictionary always reflects current truth
portable - the same idea, the same names, across hosts
tight - timing is exposed at audio rate so the patch can stay in lock-step with the session

vignette.p: The package is the Max-side counterpart of a broader VOID Link Audio effort that also targets TouchDesigner, VCV Rack, openFrameworks, and plug-in formats.

ul.li: void.linkaudio.send~ - publish audio + expose timing
void.linkaudio.receive~ - receive audio + expose timing
